011.rationale: Military officers blend digital command/control systems with physical operations, strategic planning, and real-time human leadership in unpredictable environments. While AI can enhance intelligence analysis, logistics optimization, and some planning functions, core military leadership requires human judgment in life-or-death situations with high uncertainty.
011.regulatory_friction: Military operations fall under specialized defense regulations beyond civilian AI Act provisions, but any AI systems affecting personnel decisions (promotions, assignments) would trigger works council consultation requirements. National security considerations create additional barriers to AI adoption in sensitive command functions.
key_vulnerable_tasks: analyse potential threats against national security

021.rationale: Non-commissioned officers operate in highly dynamic, unstructured environments requiring real-time tactical decisions, physical presence, and human leadership under extreme conditions. While AI can assist with intelligence analysis and threat identification, core duties like squad leadership, combat operations, and split-second tactical decisions in chaotic environments remain largely beyond current AI capabilities.
021.regulatory_friction: Military operations fall under national security exemptions from most EU regulations including the AI Act and GDPR. Additionally, the life-or-death nature of combat operations and strict military command structures create institutional barriers to AI adoption in core operational roles.

031.rationale: Armed forces other ranks involve primarily physical, high-stakes operations requiring real-time decision-making in unstructured environments with significant safety implications. While some roles like intelligence communications have digital components that AI could assist with, the core functions demand physical presence, manual dexterity, and human judgment in life-or-death situations.
031.regulatory_friction: Military operations are subject to extremely strict command structures, national security protocols, and international laws of armed conflict that severely limit AI adoption. The life-or-death nature of military decisions requires human accountability that regulatory frameworks cannot delegate to AI systems.

111.rationale: Legislators and senior officials have moderate AI exposure primarily through research, policy analysis, and administrative support tasks. AI can assist with analyzing legislation, economic trends, drafting documents, and managing information flows, but core functions require political judgment, stakeholder relationship management, and public representation that remain fundamentally human.
111.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates massive barriers as AI systems for public administration and governance would be classified as high-risk, requiring extensive oversight. Democratic accountability principles, constitutional requirements for human decision-making in governance, and strong employment protections for public officials create nearly insurmountable regulatory barriers to AI displacement.

112.rationale: Managing directors and chief executives work with highly digital information flows - financial data, strategic reports, market analysis, and performance metrics that AI can process and synthesize effectively. AI can automate much of the data analysis, reporting, and even strategic recommendation generation that forms the foundation of executive decision-making.
112.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates significant barriers as executive decisions often involve high-risk AI applications in workforce management and performance evaluation. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI systems affecting working conditions, substantially slowing adoption of AI-powered management tools.

121.rationale: Business services and administration managers perform largely digital work including strategic planning, financial analysis, compliance monitoring, and process optimization that AI can significantly enhance or automate. Many core tasks involve data analysis, report generation, policy development, and administrative coordination that are highly susceptible to AI transformation.
121.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates high-risk classifications for HR managers and talent acquisition managers in recruitment and performance evaluation. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for any AI affecting working conditions, significantly slowing adoption across all management functions.

122.rationale: Sales, marketing and development managers work extensively with digital tools, data analysis, content creation, and strategic planning - all areas where AI excels. AI can automate market research, consumer trend analysis, campaign optimization, content generation, and predictive analytics for product development.
122.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates barriers for AI systems used in recruitment and performance evaluation of teams. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for AI tools affecting working conditions, and GDPR limits automated decision-making in customer profiling and targeting.

131.rationale: Production managers in agriculture, forestry and fisheries work in a mixed digital-physical environment where AI can significantly enhance planning, monitoring, and optimization tasks through data analysis and predictive modeling. However, the role requires substantial field presence, hands-on management of living systems, and real-time decision-making in unstructured outdoor environments that limit full automation potential.
131.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a create consultation requirements for AI systems affecting workforce management and working conditions. GDPR constraints on automated decision-making also slow adoption of AI tools for performance evaluation and resource allocation decisions.
key_protected_tasks: hands-on problem solving in field conditions

132.rationale: These managers perform largely digital tasks including planning, scheduling, forecasting, supply chain optimization, and data analysis that AI can significantly enhance or automate. However, they require substantial human judgment for strategic decisions, stakeholder management, and handling complex operational exceptions in physical environments.
132.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting workforce management and performance evaluation. GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making limit AI deployment in personnel and operational decisions.

133.rationale: ICT service managers work heavily with digital systems, data analysis, and strategic planning - all areas where AI excels. AI can automate documentation processes, analyze system performance metrics, generate project reports, and support strategic decision-making through data insights. However, senior leadership roles require complex stakeholder management, strategic vision, and accountability that still need human oversight.
133.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for workforce management systems and performance evaluation create significant barriers, as these managers often implement AI systems affecting employees. Works council co-determination rights require consultation when introducing AI tools that change working conditions, slowing adoption in management roles.
key_vulnerable_tasks: environmental impact assessment of ICT resources
key_protected_tasks: strategic vision setting and organizational leadership
budget approval and resource allocation decisions

134.rationale: Professional services managers operate across highly digitized workflows including strategic planning, resource allocation, performance monitoring, and administrative coordination - all areas where AI can provide significant analytical support and automation. Many core management functions like budget analysis, staff scheduling, policy drafting, and operational reporting are well-suited to AI augmentation.
134.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates major barriers as many managers oversee recruitment, performance evaluation, and workforce management systems. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI affecting working conditions, while employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring in management roles.

141.rationale: Hotel and restaurant managers have significant digital workflow components (scheduling, inventory, financial analysis, marketing) that AI can automate, plus AI can optimize operations like revenue management and staff allocation. However, core leadership functions requiring real-time human interaction, relationship building, and crisis management in dynamic physical environments create substantial protection barriers.
141.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for AI systems affecting staff scheduling and performance management. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in employee evaluation and customer service contexts.
key_vulnerable_tasks: coordinate activities across hospitality rooms division
design indicators for food waste reduction

142.rationale: Retail and wholesale trade managers perform many digitally-native tasks including inventory management, sales analytics, budgeting, scheduling, and supplier coordination that AI can significantly enhance or automate. AI can optimize pricing, predict demand, automate ordering, and provide customer insights, while digital tools increasingly handle administrative workflows.
142.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications around workforce management systems and German/Austrian works council requirements for AI affecting employee scheduling, performance monitoring, or working conditions create significant barriers. GDPR also restricts automated customer profiling and decision-making systems.
key_protected_tasks: physical store layout and merchandising decisions

143.rationale: Other services managers work extensively with digital systems for operations management, scheduling, budgeting, data analysis, and customer service coordination. AI can automate much of their analytical work, resource optimization, staff scheduling, and performance monitoring across diverse service sectors.
143.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting staff scheduling and performance management. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making for customer profiling and staff evaluation in gambling, tourism, and recreational facilities.

211.rationale: Physical and earth science professionals have moderate-high AI exposure due to substantial data analysis, pattern recognition, and modeling tasks that AI excels at. However, core research design, field work, specialized instrumentation, and complex scientific interpretation still require significant human expertise.
211.regulatory_friction: GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making affect AI use in environmental assessments and policy recommendations. Works council consultation requirements apply when AI tools change research workflows or performance evaluation methods in institutional settings.

212.rationale: This occupation group works almost entirely with digital data, mathematical models, and statistical analysis - core strengths of AI systems. AI can already perform sophisticated statistical analysis, predictive modeling, and pattern recognition that forms the foundation of actuarial and statistical work.
212.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making significantly impact actuarial and statistical work used for insurance pricing, risk assessment, and financial advice. Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG §87 apply when AI systems change analytical workflows and job responsibilities.

213.rationale: Life science professionals have significant digital components including data analysis, research synthesis, report writing, and computational modeling that AI can enhance or automate. However, fieldwork, laboratory experimentation, sample collection, and regulatory compliance oversight require physical presence and specialized judgment that limit full automation.
213.regulatory_friction: GDPR restrictions on automated environmental compliance decisions and employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring. Works council consultation requirements apply when AI systems affect working conditions in research institutions and environmental agencies.

214.rationale: Engineering professionals work extensively with digital tools (CAD, simulation software, modeling) and produce digital deliverables that AI can significantly augment or automate. AI can assist with design optimization, calculations, compliance checking, documentation, and predictive analysis across most engineering disciplines.
214.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination under BetrVG §87 requires consultation when AI systems affect working conditions or performance evaluation. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in engineering assessments that affect individuals or projects.

215.rationale: Electrotechnology engineers work extensively with digital tools (CAD, simulation software, modeling) and produce deliverables that are largely digital, making them highly exposed to AI assistance and automation. AI can significantly enhance design processes, simulation modeling, testing protocols, and technical documentation, while also enabling predictive maintenance and system optimization tasks.
215.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation when AI systems affect engineering working conditions or performance evaluation. GDPR constraints on automated decision-making may limit AI's role in design approval processes and system recommendations.

216.rationale: This occupation group has extremely high AI exposure as the vast majority of work is digital/computer-based, involving tasks AI excels at: image generation, 3D modeling, layout design, and visual content creation. Current AI tools like DALL-E, Midjourney, and emerging 3D AI systems can directly automate or dramatically accelerate core tasks across design disciplines.
216.regulatory_friction: Limited regulatory friction as most design work doesn't trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications. Some friction from general employment protections and potential GDPR constraints when AI tools process client data in design workflows.

221.rationale: Medical doctors work with significant digital components (diagnostic imaging, lab results, medical records) that AI can enhance, but core work requires complex clinical reasoning, physical examination skills, and real-time patient interaction in unpredictable scenarios. AI can assist with diagnosis and treatment recommendations but cannot fully replace the human judgment required for patient care.
221.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems for healthcare as high-risk, requiring conformity assessments and human oversight. Medical device regulations and professional licensing create additional barriers to AI adoption in clinical decision-making.
key_vulnerable_tasks: analyze lab results and medical data
research medical literature and treatment protocols

222.rationale: Nursing and midwifery involve significant physical care, real-time human interaction, and manual dexterity that create natural barriers to AI automation. However, AI can assist with documentation, care planning, medication management, and diagnostic support tools.
222.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies healthcare AI as high-risk, requiring conformity assessments and human oversight. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated medical decision-making, while works councils must approve AI systems affecting nursing workflows and performance evaluation.
key_protected_tasks: complex diagnostic assessments requiring human judgment

223.rationale: Traditional and complementary medicine professionals require significant hands-on physical assessment, direct patient interaction, and manual therapeutic techniques (acupuncture, massage, aromatherapy application) that AI cannot replicate. While AI could assist with initial assessments, treatment planning, and record-keeping, the core therapeutic interventions require human touch, intuition, and real-time adaptation to patient responses.
223.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications around healthcare AI systems create substantial barriers, requiring human oversight for medical decisions. GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated healthcare decision-making, combined with strict medical device regulations, significantly limit AI adoption in direct patient care roles.

225.rationale: Veterinary work combines high technical knowledge with significant hands-on physical examination, surgery, and direct animal interaction. AI can assist with diagnostic imaging analysis, treatment recommendations, and administrative tasks, but the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time assessment of living animals in unstructured environments.
225.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates constraints around automated health decisions, while strong employment protections in veterinary practices slow workforce restructuring. The EU AI Act's high-risk classifications don't directly apply, but medical device regulations govern AI diagnostic tools.

226.rationale: Other health professionals work with mixed digital-physical tasks, where AI can significantly assist in diagnostics, image analysis, treatment planning, and clinical decision support. However, these roles require specialized clinical judgment, direct patient interaction, and manual procedures that create natural barriers to full automation.
226.regulatory_friction: Healthcare AI systems face strict EU AI Act high-risk classifications for medical devices and health applications, requiring extensive validation and approval processes. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in healthcare, while strong employment protections in healthcare sectors slow workforce restructuring.
key_protected_tasks: complex clinical decision-making requiring human judgment

231.rationale: University teachers work heavily with digital content creation, research, grading, and knowledge synthesis - all areas where AI excels. AI can assist with lecture preparation, automated grading, research analysis, and content generation, though the role requires complex human judgment in pedagogy and original research.
231.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems in education as high-risk, requiring strict compliance and human oversight. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in student evaluation, while works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI affecting academic working conditions.

232.rationale: Vocational education teachers have high AI exposure because much of their work involves creating curricula, lesson plans, assessments, and educational materials - fundamentally digital work products that AI can generate effectively. AI can also provide personalized learning experiences, simulate training scenarios, and deliver standardized theoretical content across many vocational domains.
232.regulatory_friction: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems in education as high-risk (Annex III), requiring strict compliance measures. Works council co-determination rights mean any AI affecting teaching methods or performance evaluation requires consultation, while strong employment protections in education slow workforce restructuring.

233.rationale: Secondary education teachers have moderate-high technical exposure as AI can assist with lesson planning, content creation, assessment design, and grading. However, the core teaching function requires real-time human interaction, classroom management, and adaptation to individual student needs in physical environments.
233.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems in education as high-risk, requiring conformity assessments and human oversight. Works council co-determination rights require consultation on any AI affecting teaching conditions or student evaluation methods.

234.rationale: Primary and early childhood teachers have moderate AI exposure as their work involves substantial digital content creation (lesson plans, assessments, educational materials) and administrative tasks that AI can enhance or automate. However, the core teaching function requires real-time human interaction, emotional intelligence, and adaptive responses to diverse student needs in physical classroom environments.
234.regulatory_friction: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems in education as high-risk (Annex III), requiring strict compliance and human oversight. Works council consultation rights under German BetrVG §87 and Austrian ArbVG for any AI affecting working conditions, combined with strong teacher employment protections, create substantial barriers to AI-driven workforce restructuring.

235.rationale: This occupation group has high technical exposure because many roles involve digital content creation (e-learning development, curriculum design, assessment), knowledge transfer that AI can increasingly support, and administrative tasks that are highly automatable. However, the core teaching function still requires human interaction, emotional intelligence, and real-time adaptation to individual student needs.
235.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems in education as high-risk, requiring extensive compliance measures. Works council co-determination rights mean any AI deployment affecting teaching conditions requires consultation, while GDPR restricts automated student assessment and profiling.

241.rationale: Finance professionals work primarily with digital data, financial models, and analytical tasks that AI excels at - pattern recognition in markets, automated reporting, risk calculations, and document analysis. Many routine tasks like basic auditing, financial statement preparation, and standard investment analysis are highly susceptible to AI automation.
241.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III covers AI systems for creditworthiness assessment and loan decisions, while GDPR Article 22 restricts automated financial decision-making. Strong employment protections and works council rights in countries like Germany slow workforce restructuring in the heavily regulated financial sector.

242.rationale: Administration professionals work extensively with digital information processing, data analysis, policy research, and report writing - all areas where AI excels. Many tasks involve synthesizing information, creating frameworks, and producing structured outputs that AI can handle effectively.
242.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates significant barriers for HR officers and recruitment consultants performing workforce management. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, while GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in policy and administrative contexts.

243.rationale: This occupation group has high AI exposure as most work is digitally-driven, involving content creation, data analysis, customer communication, and strategic planning that AI can increasingly automate or augment. AI can generate marketing copy, analyze market data, create customer personas, optimize media buying, and automate many sales processes.
243.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates barriers for AI systems used in recruitment and workforce management (affecting HR-related marketing roles). GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in customer profiling and targeting, while works council consultation requirements slow adoption of AI tools that affect working conditions.
key_protected_tasks: complex B2B technical sales requiring deep expertise
regulatory and policy advocacy requiring human credibility

251.rationale: This occupation group represents the highest technical AI exposure - work is entirely digital/computer-based, involves tasks like code generation, testing, and analysis that AI excels at. AI coding assistants already demonstrate significant capability in software development, and emerging AI systems can perform many analytical and architectural tasks.
251.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates constraints around automated decision-making in system design and data processing roles. Works council co-determination rights require consultation when AI tools affect working conditions or productivity metrics for development teams.

252.rationale: Database and network professionals work entirely in digital environments with highly structured, rule-based tasks that AI can increasingly automate - from network configuration and monitoring to database optimization and security threat detection. AI code generation, automated system administration, and intelligent network management tools are rapidly advancing to handle routine maintenance, troubleshooting, and even architectural decisions.
252.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making limit AI deployment in access control and user management systems. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation when AI tools change working conditions or monitoring practices in IT departments.

261.rationale: Legal professionals work heavily with digital documents, legal research, contract analysis, and regulatory compliance - all areas where AI excels. AI can draft contracts, analyze case law, perform due diligence, and automate regulatory compliance checks. However, core judicial functions, client counseling, and courtroom advocacy require human judgment and interaction.
261.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates high-risk classifications for AI systems used in law enforcement and access to essential services (affecting prosecutors, judges). Works council co-determination under BetrVG §87 requires consultation for AI systems affecting legal professionals' working conditions, while GDPR Article 22 restricts automated legal decision-making.
key_vulnerable_tasks: legal research and case law analysis

262.rationale: This occupation involves substantial digital work including cataloging, database management, research queries, and information organization that AI can perform well. However, physical handling of artifacts, specialized conservation techniques, and nuanced curatorial judgment requiring deep cultural/historical context provide moderate protection.
262.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated processing of cultural and historical data, plus strong EU employment protections in public cultural institutions slow workforce restructuring. Works council co-determination rights in cultural organizations require consultation for AI systems affecting cataloging and collection management workflows.
key_protected_tasks: animal care coordination requiring real-time judgment

263.rationale: This occupation group has significant AI exposure due to extensive documentation, research, and analytical work that is increasingly digital. AI can assist with case documentation, research synthesis, initial assessments, and data analysis for economists and social researchers.
263.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III heavily regulates AI in essential services access and law enforcement contexts where many social workers operate. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in sensitive social services, and works council rights apply to AI affecting social worker performance evaluation and case management systems.
key_protected_tasks: building trust relationships with vulnerable clients

264.rationale: This occupation group works almost entirely with digital content - writing, editing, translating, and creating text-based materials that AI can directly process and generate. AI has demonstrated strong capabilities in content creation, translation, editing, and even specialized writing tasks, making this group highly exposed to both automation and productivity-driven displacement.
264.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making may limit AI use in editorial decisions and content curation. Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG §87 could slow implementation of AI tools that change working conditions in media organizations.
key_protected_tasks: complex investigative journalism requiring human judgment
cultural adaptation requiring deep contextual understanding

265.rationale: Creative and performing artists face significant AI disruption in digital content creation (storyboard artists, video editors, composers) and administrative tasks, but live performance, physical artistry, and human emotional connection provide substantial protection. AI can generate music, visual art, and assist in post-production, but struggles with live audience interaction and embodied physical performance.
265.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act provisions around automated content creation and GDPR restrictions on AI-generated creative works create uncertainty around copyright and attribution. Employment protection laws slow industry restructuring despite technological capability.

311.rationale: This occupation group involves significant digital work (CAD, 3D modeling, data analysis, technical documentation) that AI can automate or dramatically enhance, but also requires physical inspection, hands-on testing, and equipment maintenance that creates natural barriers. The mix of digital design work and physical technical tasks places it in moderate-high exposure territory.
311.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 and ArbVG §96a will slow AI adoption affecting working conditions, while safety-critical roles (aviation, nuclear, construction inspection) face additional regulatory oversight that constrains automation deployment.

312.rationale: These supervisors manage complex physical production processes requiring real-time decision-making, safety oversight, and human coordination in unstructured environments. While AI can assist with scheduling, reporting, and process optimization, the core supervisory functions require physical presence, contextual judgment, and immediate response to safety situations that limit pure automation potential.
312.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 create significant barriers since AI systems for workforce scheduling and performance monitoring require consultation. GDPR restrictions on automated workforce management decisions further constrain AI deployment in supervisory roles.
key_vulnerable_tasks: preparing production reports and cost analysis
key_protected_tasks: real-time safety oversight and emergency response
on-site quality control and compliance verification

313.rationale: Process control technicians work extensively with digital control systems, monitoring interfaces, and data analysis - areas where AI excels. Many tasks involve pattern recognition, predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and optimization decisions that AI systems can perform effectively. However, the role requires physical presence for safety oversight and emergency response in critical infrastructure.
313.regulatory_friction: Critical infrastructure sectors (energy, water, waste) face stringent safety regulations that slow AI adoption. Works councils have strong co-determination rights over AI systems affecting working conditions in industrial settings, and employment protection laws make workforce restructuring difficult in these traditionally unionized sectors.

314.rationale: Life science technicians perform substantial digital work including data analysis, report compilation, and research support that AI can automate or significantly enhance. However, hands-on laboratory work, field sampling, equipment maintenance, and real-time biological monitoring require physical presence and manual dexterity that creates natural barriers to full automation.
314.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated decision-making in research contexts and employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring. Works council consultation requirements apply when AI systems change working conditions in laboratory environments.

315.rationale: This occupation group combines highly regulated safety-critical roles with mixed AI exposure. Some functions like flight planning, route optimization, and maintenance scheduling are highly amenable to AI assistance, while real-time piloting, emergency response, and safety-critical decision-making require human judgment and are heavily regulated.
315.regulatory_friction: Aviation and maritime operations fall under strict safety regulations where AI systems would trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications for safety-critical applications. Additionally, international aviation (ICAO) and maritime (IMO) standards create multilayered regulatory barriers beyond EU jurisdiction.

321.rationale: Medical and pharmaceutical technicians perform a mix of routine analytical tasks (sample processing, equipment monitoring, data entry) that AI can increasingly automate, but also require hands-on technical procedures, patient interaction, and real-time clinical decision-making in physical healthcare environments. While laboratory data analysis and basic administrative functions are highly automatable, the physical manipulation of medical equipment, direct patient care, and safety-critical procedures create natural barriers to full AI replacement.
321.regulatory_friction: Healthcare AI systems face strict EU Medical Device Regulation requirements, GDPR constraints on patient data processing, and works council consultation for any AI affecting technicians' working conditions. The EU AI Act's high-risk classification for healthcare applications creates additional approval barriers for AI systems used in clinical settings.
key_protected_tasks: real-time clinical monitoring and emergency response

322.rationale: Maternity support workers require extensive physical presence, hands-on care, real-time human interaction during emotionally sensitive moments, and work in unstructured healthcare environments. While AI could assist with documentation and basic monitoring, the core tasks involve direct patient care, physical assistance during childbirth, and emotional support that require human presence and empathy.
322.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for healthcare AI systems create high-risk requirements, GDPR restricts automated healthcare decisions, and works council consultation rights slow adoption of AI systems affecting working conditions in healthcare settings.
key_protected_tasks: emotional support and empathy during labor

323.rationale: Herbal therapists blend digital tasks (research, treatment planning, record-keeping) with essential human elements (physical examination, therapeutic relationships, holistic assessment). AI can assist with plant properties research, treatment suggestions, and administrative tasks, but the personalized, relationship-based nature of complementary medicine and need for physical presence during consultations creates significant barriers.
323.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for healthcare decision-making systems create barriers to AI-assisted diagnosis and treatment recommendations. GDPR automated decision-making restrictions further limit AI's role in health-related assessments and treatment planning.

324.rationale: Veterinary technicians work in mixed digital-physical environments with some administrative tasks (record keeping, appointment scheduling) highly susceptible to AI, but core responsibilities require hands-on animal care, physical examination skills, and real-time assessment of animal behavior and distress. AI can assist with diagnostic support and documentation but cannot replace the tactile, observational, and manual dexterity requirements of animal handling and treatment administration.
324.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act provisions around healthcare applications and GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making create moderate barriers for AI diagnostic tools. Employment protection laws and potential works council involvement in AI adoption for workforce management add friction.
key_protected_tasks: direct client counseling on animal welfare

325.rationale: This occupation group spans highly varied roles from emergency paramedics requiring real-time physical intervention to administrative roles like medical records clerks with predominantly digital workflows. AI can significantly impact documentation, coding, scheduling, and some diagnostic support tasks, but many roles require physical presence, manual dexterity, and critical human judgment in emergency situations.
325.regulatory_friction: Healthcare AI systems face strict EU AI Act high-risk classifications for medical device applications and patient care decisions. GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated healthcare decisions, plus medical device regulations and professional licensing requirements create substantial barriers to AI adoption in direct patient care roles.
key_protected_tasks: emergency medical response and paramedic care
complex safety inspections requiring physical assessment
manual assembly and fitting of optical devices

331.rationale: This occupation group involves highly digital work products including financial analysis, risk assessment, data processing, and advisory services that AI excels at. Most tasks involve pattern recognition in financial data, automated decision-making, and generating analytical reports - all core AI strengths with documented high performance.
331.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems for creditworthiness assessment and access to essential financial services as high-risk, requiring extensive compliance. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in financial services, while works councils must be consulted on AI systems affecting working conditions.
key_protected_tasks: high-stakes financial advisory requiring human judgment

332.rationale: Sales and purchasing agents operate heavily through digital channels, conducting market analysis, price comparisons, and client communications that AI can increasingly automate. AI excels at pattern recognition for risk assessment, market trend analysis, and matching buyers with sellers across commodity and insurance markets.
332.regulatory_friction: GDPR restricts automated decision-making in insurance underwriting and client profiling, while works councils must be consulted on AI systems affecting sales performance evaluation and commission structures.
key_vulnerable_tasks: lead generation and initial client outreach
key_protected_tasks: physical inspection of properties and goods

333.rationale: Business services agents perform predominantly digital, information-intensive tasks like contract analysis, market research, client communication, and document processing that AI can effectively automate. Many roles involve pattern recognition, data analysis, and routine administrative work that current AI systems handle well.
333.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for recruitment (employment agents) and GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making in client profiling create moderate barriers. Works council consultation requirements for AI affecting working conditions add procedural delays.

334.rationale: Administrative and specialised secretaries work with fundamentally digital outputs - documentation, correspondence, data entry, scheduling, and process coordination. AI can automate most administrative tasks including document generation, data analysis, scheduling optimization, and routine correspondence management.
334.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III applies to workforce management systems that could automate performance evaluation and task assignment. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for AI systems affecting administrative working conditions, while GDPR restricts automated processing of employee and client data.

335.rationale: Regulatory government work involves substantial digital tasks like document review, case analysis, benefit calculations, and compliance checking that AI can handle well. However, the occupation requires legal judgment, stakeholder interaction, and enforcement decisions that limit full automation.
335.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems for 'access to essential public services and benefits' as high-risk, requiring extensive compliance. Works council co-determination rights and strong employment protections in government roles create additional barriers to AI deployment.

341.rationale: This occupation group shows moderate AI exposure due to mixed digital/physical nature. Legal assistants, case administrators, and court clerks handle substantial documentation and research that AI can automate, while care workers require physical presence and human interaction that limits automation. Administrative and analytical tasks across the group are highly vulnerable to AI transformation.
341.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates major barriers for AI systems used in social services, law enforcement support, and access to essential services. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI affecting care worker scheduling or performance evaluation, while GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decisions about vulnerable populations.

342.rationale: Sports and fitness workers require extensive physical presence, real-time human interaction, manual dexterity for demonstrations, and work in unstructured physical environments. While AI can assist with program design, progress tracking, and basic instructional content, the core functions demand hands-on coaching, safety supervision, and adaptive physical guidance that AI cannot replicate.
342.regulatory_friction: GDPR restrictions on automated processing of health/fitness data and works council consultation requirements for any AI systems affecting working conditions create moderate barriers. Additionally, safety-critical roles like lifeguards face heightened scrutiny for AI-assisted decision making.
key_protected_tasks: adapt teaching to individual capabilities in real-time

343.rationale: This diverse occupation group has high variability in AI exposure. Digital aspects (photography processing, design visualization, administrative tasks, content creation) are highly automatable, while physical craft work, live performance coordination, and creative direction require significant human judgment and presence. AI can assist with design generation, image editing, menu planning, and documentation but cannot replace the manual dexterity, real-time coordination, and creative intuition central to most roles.
343.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates some constraints around automated decision-making in hiring/casting decisions. Works council consultation may be required when AI systems change working conditions in larger cultural institutions or restaurant chains.
key_protected_tasks: manual craft work (prop making, set building)

351.rationale: ICT operations and support work is heavily digital with many tasks amenable to AI automation including troubleshooting, system monitoring, routine maintenance, and user support through chatbots. AI can handle many Level 1 support tickets, automate network diagnostics, and assist with system administration tasks.
351.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, slowing deployment of automated support systems. GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making also limit AI's role in security and access management decisions.
key_protected_tasks: strategic IT planning and architecture decisions

352.rationale: This occupation group combines highly digital work (sound editing, mastering, telecommunications system management) with real-time performance control and physical equipment operation. AI can automate many post-production tasks, system monitoring, and basic editing functions, but creative decision-making and live performance coordination require human expertise.
352.regulatory_friction: Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG §87 create friction when AI tools affect working conditions in broadcast facilities. GDPR automated decision-making restrictions may limit AI systems in telecommunications network management and performance evaluation contexts.
key_protected_tasks: live performance video operation and creative timing
artistic sound design and creative concept development
real-time coordination with performers and production crew
hands-on equipment repair and physical troubleshooting

411.rationale: General office clerks perform predominantly digital, routine administrative tasks that are highly susceptible to AI automation. Their work involves data entry, document processing, scheduling, and basic customer communication - all areas where current AI excels and can achieve near-human performance.
411.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making limit AI handling of customer interactions and membership decisions. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation before implementing AI systems that change working conditions or could lead to workforce restructuring.

412.rationale: Secretarial work is fundamentally digital and document-based, making it highly susceptible to AI automation. Tasks like email drafting, scheduling, document management, and basic administrative workflows can be largely automated by current AI systems. The work involves routine information processing with clear patterns that AI excels at.
412.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, slowing implementation. GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making create additional barriers for AI systems managing personnel schedules and internal communications.
key_protected_tasks: provide contextual judgment in complex situations

413.rationale: Keyboard operators perform purely digital, routine tasks that are ideal targets for AI automation. Data entry, typing, and basic data processing are already being automated by AI systems, and LLMs can handle document compilation, formatting, and basic data verification with high accuracy.
413.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 may slow automation where data entry involves automated decision-making about individuals. Works council consultation requirements apply when AI systems change working conditions, but the regulatory barriers are relatively minor for these routine clerical tasks.
key_protected_tasks: complex data interpretation requiring domain expertise
handling sensitive documents requiring human judgment

421.rationale: This occupation group involves heavily digital, rule-based transactions and calculations that AI can automate effectively. Tasks like odds compilation, account processing, payment handling, and customer information services are highly susceptible to AI automation given their structured, data-driven nature.
421.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making affect credit assessments and debt collection. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 create consultation requirements for AI systems affecting teller workflows and performance monitoring.
key_vulnerable_tasks: carry out end of day accounts

422.rationale: Client information workers perform largely digital, information-based tasks that AI can handle effectively - answering routine queries, processing reservations, conducting surveys, and providing standardized information. Many roles like call center operators, live chat agents, and ticket sales are already being automated by chatbots and AI systems.
422.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in customer service contexts, while works council co-determination rights require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions. Emergency dispatching may trigger AI Act high-risk classifications due to safety implications.
key_protected_tasks: emergency medical dispatching requiring critical judgment
complex problem resolution requiring human empathy
handling sensitive customer complaints requiring discretion

431.rationale: Numerical clerks perform highly digitized, rule-based tasks like data entry, calculations, reconciliations, and document processing that are ideal targets for AI automation. Their work involves pattern recognition, mathematical computations, and structured data manipulation - core AI strengths that can dramatically reduce workforce needs.
431.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making limit AI deployment in financial transactions and insurance processing. Works council co-determination rights in Germany/Austria require consultation before implementing AI systems that affect working conditions, slowing adoption timelines.

432.rationale: Material-recording and transport clerks work extensively with digital systems for scheduling, tracking, and coordinating logistics operations. AI can automate route optimization, cargo scheduling, inventory management, and regulatory compliance documentation. However, real-time safety-critical decisions and physical coordination of vessels/aircraft require human oversight.
432.regulatory_friction: Transportation safety regulations require human oversight for critical operations like air traffic control and maritime piloting. Works councils have co-determination rights over AI systems affecting scheduling and performance monitoring of transport workers.

441.rationale: This occupation group involves highly digital tasks like document processing, correspondence, CV screening, and administrative coordination that AI can readily automate. Many core functions like proofreading, foreign language correspondence, and HR screening are prime targets for AI systems.
441.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies HR recruitment and workforce management as high-risk, requiring conformity assessments for HR assistants' CV screening functions. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation before implementing AI systems that affect working conditions.
key_protected_tasks: nuanced human judgment in HR assessments

511.rationale: Travel attendants, conductors and guides require significant physical presence, real-time human interaction, and manual safety tasks that create natural barriers to AI automation. While some informational and educational content can be enhanced by AI, core responsibilities like emergency response, physical safety supervision, and in-person passenger assistance remain largely human-dependent.
511.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for workforce management systems affect scheduling and performance evaluation of these workers. GDPR restricts automated passenger profiling and service decisions, while strong employment protections slow workforce restructuring in transportation sectors.

512.rationale: Cooking involves significant physical manipulation, real-time sensory evaluation (taste, smell, texture), and adaptation to variable ingredients and equipment in unstructured kitchen environments. While AI can assist with recipe optimization and inventory management, the core tasks require manual dexterity, sensory judgment, and physical presence that current AI cannot replicate.
512.regulatory_friction: GDPR may restrict AI systems used for workforce scheduling and performance monitoring in commercial kitchens. Works council consultation requirements could slow adoption of AI-driven kitchen management systems that affect working conditions or staffing decisions.

513.rationale: Waiters and bartenders work in highly physical, real-time customer service environments requiring manual dexterity, immediate human interaction, and navigation of unstructured physical spaces. While AI can assist with some tasks like order taking or recommendations, the core work involves physical service delivery, emotional intelligence, and handling unpredictable customer needs that require human presence.
513.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated customer profiling and recommendation systems create some friction. Works council consultation requirements for any AI systems affecting working conditions (like automated scheduling or performance monitoring) add regulatory barriers.
key_protected_tasks: handling complex customer complaints and special requests

514.rationale: Hairdressing and beauty services require intensive manual dexterity, real-time tactile feedback, and in-person physical manipulation of hair, skin, and body treatments. While AI could assist with appointment scheduling and basic style recommendations, the core work involves hands-on physical skills that current AI cannot replicate.
514.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated customer profiling and recommendation systems, plus general employment protections that slow workforce restructuring. Works council consultation requirements would apply to any AI systems affecting salon working conditions or performance evaluation.

515.rationale: Building and housekeeping supervisors have moderate AI exposure through digital administrative tasks like scheduling, inventory management, and basic accounting, but core responsibilities require physical presence, manual work, and real-time problem-solving in unstructured environments. AI can enhance efficiency in planning and coordination but cannot replace the hands-on nature of cleaning, maintenance, security monitoring, and direct customer service.
515.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions like scheduling or performance monitoring. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated workforce management decisions, slowing adoption of AI tools for staff supervision and evaluation.
key_protected_tasks: direct customer service and relationship management

516.rationale: This occupation group involves predominantly hands-on physical work with animals, face-to-face human interaction, and services requiring physical presence. While some administrative tasks (scheduling, record-keeping, basic customer communication) could be automated, the core functions require manual dexterity, real-time animal handling, and in-person human services that AI cannot directly perform.
516.regulatory_friction: GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making may limit AI use in customer-facing roles like dating consultants or funeral directors. Employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring, though the physical nature of most roles means less AI regulation applies.

521.rationale: Street and market vendors require significant physical presence, face-to-face customer interaction, manual food preparation, and operation in unstructured outdoor environments. While AI could assist with inventory management or payment processing, the core work involves tactile food handling, real-time personal sales interactions, and adaptation to dynamic street/market conditions that AI cannot replicate.
521.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates minor friction around automated payment systems and customer data collection. General employment protection laws may slow any workforce restructuring, though this occupation has limited formal employment relationships.

522.rationale: Shop salespersons have moderate AI exposure as AI can handle product recommendations, inventory management, and basic customer inquiries through chatbots or recommendation systems. However, the role requires significant physical presence, hands-on product demonstration, complex customer relationship building, and real-time problem solving in unstructured retail environments.
522.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restricts automated customer profiling and pricing decisions, while works council consultation requirements (BetrVG §87) apply to AI systems affecting sales targets and performance monitoring. General employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring in retail.

523.rationale: Cashiers and ticket clerks perform highly routine, transaction-based tasks that are prime targets for AI automation. Self-checkout systems, automated ticketing kiosks, and digital payment processing can directly replace most core functions including payment processing, inventory tracking, and basic customer service interactions.
523.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making may slow deployment of AI systems that make determinations about customer transactions or eligibility. Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG §87 will create friction when employers implement AI systems that affect cashier working conditions or staffing levels.
key_vulnerable_tasks: operate cash register and process payments
key_protected_tasks: assist customers with complex special needs
provide personalized service for high-value transactions

524.rationale: This occupation group shows high AI vulnerability due to substantial digital components - call centre agents can be replaced by AI chatbots, rental transactions can be automated, and many sales processes involve routine digital tasks like documentation, payment processing, and customer service that AI handles well. The physical presence requirements for models and some in-person sales create moderate protection.
524.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in customer interactions and rental approvals. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation before implementing AI systems that change working conditions in call centres and rental operations.

531.rationale: Child care workers and teachers' aides perform primarily physical, relational work requiring real-time human interaction, emotional support, and physical care tasks that AI cannot replicate. While some administrative tasks like progress tracking could be AI-assisted, the core functions involve hands-on childcare, behavioral management, and individualized attention in unstructured environments.
531.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems in education as high-risk, requiring strict compliance and limiting deployment. Works council co-determination rights in educational settings create additional barriers to AI adoption affecting working conditions.

532.rationale: Personal care workers perform predominantly physical, hands-on tasks requiring direct human contact, manual dexterity, and real-time assessment of patient needs in unstructured environments. While some documentation and monitoring tasks could be AI-assisted, the core work involves physical care, emotional support, and immediate response to unpredictable human conditions that AI cannot directly perform.
532.regulatory_friction: GDPR restrictions on automated health data processing and strong EU employment protections slow AI adoption. Healthcare AI systems face additional medical device regulations and works council consultation requirements for any workforce management changes.

541.rationale: Protective services workers require physical presence, real-time human judgment in unpredictable environments, and manual dexterity for emergency response. While AI can assist with surveillance monitoring and basic threat analysis, the core functions of physical protection, emergency response, and law enforcement demand human intervention in unstructured, high-stakes situations.
541.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III restricts AI in law enforcement applications, and works councils must approve AI systems affecting officer working conditions. GDPR Article 22 limits automated decision-making in security screening and threat assessment contexts.

611.rationale: Market gardening and crop growing involves substantial physical work in unstructured outdoor environments, manual dexterity for planting/harvesting, and real-time assessment of biological systems that require human judgment. While AI can assist with some planning, monitoring, and advisory tasks, the core work remains highly physical and situational.
611.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated workforce management decisions and works council consultation requirements for AI systems affecting working conditions create moderate friction. Agricultural AI applications generally face less regulatory scrutiny than high-risk sectors.

612.rationale: Animal production involves primarily physical, hands-on tasks in unstructured outdoor environments requiring real-time assessment of animal health, behavior, and welfare. While AI can assist with monitoring systems, record-keeping, and some diagnostic support, the core work requires manual dexterity, physical presence, and intuitive animal husbandry skills that are difficult to automate.
612.regulatory_friction: EU animal welfare regulations and agricultural compliance requirements create moderate friction for AI adoption. GDPR may apply to farm management systems, and works councils could have input on AI monitoring technologies affecting working conditions.

613.rationale: Mixed crop and animal producers combine significant physical farm work with increasing digital management tasks. While AI can enhance planning, monitoring, and business management functions, the core work involves hands-on livestock care, physical crop cultivation, and real-time decision-making in unpredictable outdoor environments that limit full automation.
613.regulatory_friction: EU agricultural regulations, data protection rules for farm management systems, and employment protections for farm workers create moderate barriers. Works councils may need consultation on AI-driven workforce management tools on larger farms.
key_protected_tasks: hands-on livestock care and health assessment
real-time adaptation to weather and environmental conditions

621.rationale: Forest rangers work primarily in unstructured outdoor environments requiring physical presence, real-time situational judgment, and hands-on conservation activities. While AI can assist with some monitoring and data analysis tasks, the core work involves physical forest management, emergency response, and in-person visitor interaction that cannot be digitized.
621.regulatory_friction: Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG §87 for any AI systems affecting working conditions create moderate friction. GDPR constraints on automated decision-making may limit AI use in enforcement and safety inspection decisions.
key_protected_tasks: in-person visitor assistance and rule enforcement

622.rationale: This occupation involves predominantly physical work in unstructured outdoor environments (marine, freshwater, wilderness) requiring real-time adaptation to natural conditions, manual dexterity for equipment handling, and physical presence for animal care and extraction. While some digital tools assist with monitoring and planning, the core tasks remain heavily manual and location-dependent.
622.regulatory_friction: EU firearms legislation, environmental regulations, and animal welfare laws create compliance requirements. Works council consultation may apply to any AI monitoring systems affecting working conditions in larger aquaculture operations.
key_vulnerable_tasks: water quality monitoring and data analysis

711.rationale: Building frame and related trades workers perform highly physical, manual work requiring precise hand-eye coordination, real-time problem-solving in unstructured environments, and on-site presence. While AI can assist with design planning and material optimization, the core tasks of cutting, fitting, assembling, and installing building components require human dexterity and spatial reasoning in dynamic physical environments.
711.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 may slow adoption of AI-powered workforce management or scheduling systems. General employment protection laws create friction around workforce restructuring, though the AI Act has minimal impact since these roles don't fall under high-risk AI applications.

712.rationale: Building finishers and related trades workers perform highly physical, skilled manual work in unstructured environments requiring real-time problem-solving, precise hand-eye coordination, and adaptation to unique building conditions. While AI can assist with diagnostics, planning, and documentation, the core work involves manual installation, fitting, and repair tasks that require physical presence and dexterity that current AI cannot replicate.
712.regulatory_friction: Minimal regulatory friction as this occupation group involves physical trades work that doesn't trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications or require automated decision-making systems subject to GDPR restrictions.

713.rationale: This occupation involves primarily physical, hands-on work in unstructured environments requiring manual dexterity, real-time problem-solving, and on-site presence. While AI might assist with some planning tasks like color matching or contamination assessment, the core work of painting, cleaning, and hazardous material removal requires human physical capabilities and adaptability to varying site conditions.
713.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 may slow adoption of AI tools for workforce management or performance monitoring. Health and safety regulations for hazardous material handling (especially asbestos) create additional compliance barriers for any AI systems used in work planning or monitoring.
key_protected_tasks: manual painting with brushes and sprayers

721.rationale: These occupations involve highly physical, hands-on metalworking requiring manual dexterity, spatial reasoning, and real-time adaptation to material properties in unstructured environments. While AI can assist with design optimization and quality inspection, the core welding, molding, and assembly tasks require physical presence and tactile expertise that current AI cannot replicate.
721.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a may slow adoption of AI-assisted quality control or workforce management systems. GDPR constraints on automated performance evaluation create additional barriers for AI monitoring of work quality.

722.rationale: This occupation involves primarily physical manufacturing processes requiring manual dexterity, tactile feedback, and work with heavy machinery in unstructured industrial environments. While some CNC programming and CAD tasks have moderate AI exposure, the core work of operating metalworking equipment, handling materials, and performing precision manual adjustments creates significant barriers to AI automation.
722.regulatory_friction: Limited regulatory friction as this occupation rarely involves AI systems that trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications. Standard employment protection laws may slightly slow workforce restructuring, but GDPR and works council requirements have minimal impact on manufacturing AI adoption.
key_vulnerable_tasks: Quality control measurements and statistical analysis
key_protected_tasks: Physical handling and positioning of metal workpieces
Tactile assessment of material properties and surface quality

723.rationale: Machinery mechanics and repairers work primarily with physical systems requiring hands-on manipulation, diagnostic skills through tactile/visual inspection, and real-world problem-solving in unstructured environments. While AI can assist with diagnostic tools and maintenance scheduling, the core work involves manual dexterity and physical presence that creates natural barriers to automation.
723.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 may slow adoption of AI diagnostic tools that affect working conditions. Aircraft maintenance faces additional aviation safety regulations that require extensive certification processes for any AI systems.

731.rationale: Handicraft workers primarily engage in physical manipulation of materials requiring manual dexterity, tactile feedback, and fine motor skills that current AI systems cannot replicate. Their work involves traditional techniques, artistic judgment, and hands-on craftsmanship in unstructured environments with unique materials and custom specifications.
731.regulatory_friction: Minimal regulatory friction as this occupation operates outside high-risk AI classifications under the EU AI Act. GDPR and works council requirements have limited relevance since AI applications are minimal.
key_protected_tasks: quality assessment through touch and sight

732.rationale: Printing trades workers face high AI exposure as their work is largely digital (design software, digital printing, typesetting) and involves many automatable tasks like layout optimization, color correction, and file preparation. AI can automate prepress processes, generate print layouts, and optimize printing parameters, while advanced printing systems can operate with minimal human intervention.
732.regulatory_friction: Minimal regulatory friction as printing work doesn't trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications or significant GDPR concerns. General employment protection laws may slow workforce restructuring but won't prevent AI adoption in printing processes.

741.rationale: Electrical installers and repairers work primarily in physical environments requiring manual dexterity, real-time problem-solving in unstructured settings, and hands-on manipulation of electrical systems. While AI can assist with diagnostics, fault detection, and maintenance scheduling, the core work involves physical installation, repair, and safety-critical decision-making that requires human presence and tactile skills.
741.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 may slow adoption of AI diagnostic tools that affect working conditions or performance evaluation. Strong employment protection in EU countries creates friction around workforce restructuring even as AI tools improve productivity.

742.rationale: This occupation involves significant hands-on physical work, manual dexterity, and on-site presence that creates natural barriers to AI automation. While diagnostic tasks and some documentation could be enhanced by AI, the core work requires physical installation, repair, and maintenance in varied real-world environments that AI cannot directly perform.
742.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated decision-making and works council co-determination rights in Germany/Austria create moderate friction when AI systems are introduced for workforce management or performance monitoring. General employment protection also slows workforce restructuring.
key_protected_tasks: on-site safety assessments and emergency responses

751.rationale: Food processing trades involve significant manual dexterity, physical manipulation of products, and sensory evaluation requiring human judgment in unstructured environments. While AI can assist with some quality assessment and process optimization, the core work requires hands-on craftsmanship and real-time adaptation to variable organic materials.
751.regulatory_friction: EU food safety regulations, HACCP requirements, and employment protections create moderate friction. Works council consultation requirements for any AI systems affecting working conditions add administrative barriers to automation adoption.

752.rationale: This occupation involves primarily hands-on craftsmanship requiring manual dexterity, tactile feedback, and work in physical environments with wood materials. While some design and planning tasks could benefit from AI assistance, the core work of wood treatment, furniture restoration, and cabinet-making requires physical manipulation and real-time assessment of materials that current AI cannot perform.
752.regulatory_friction: GDPR may create minor friction if AI tools are used for customer data or automated pricing decisions. Works council consultation requirements could slow adoption of AI-assisted machinery or workflow management systems in larger workshops.

753.rationale: This occupation group spans from highly digital tasks (CAD patternmaking, 3D development) that are very AI-exposed to manual craftsmanship requiring physical dexterity and tactile skills. Digital design roles and quality inspection could see significant AI transformation, while hand sewing, upholstering, and bespoke tailoring remain protected by their manual nature.
753.regulatory_friction: GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making may limit AI adoption in quality control and grading processes. Works council consultation requirements could slow implementation of AI-assisted production systems that affect working conditions.

754.rationale: This occupation group has high technical exposure because many core tasks involve visual inspection, pattern recognition, defect detection, and data analysis - areas where AI excels. Computer vision systems can already identify defects in manufactured goods, and AI can analyze test data and grade materials more consistently than humans.
754.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 create friction when AI systems affect working conditions and performance evaluation in manufacturing settings. GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making apply to quality control decisions that could affect product approval or worker performance metrics.

811.rationale: Mining and mineral processing operators work in physically demanding environments with heavy machinery, requiring real-time coordination between automated systems and manual operations. While some monitoring, quality control, and basic administrative tasks could be enhanced by AI, the core work involves physical manipulation of materials, equipment operation in hazardous conditions, and tactile decision-making that limits full automation.
811.regulatory_friction: EU workplace safety regulations and works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) create friction when implementing AI systems that affect working conditions in high-risk mining environments. Additionally, strong employment protections slow workforce restructuring in traditional industrial sectors.
key_protected_tasks: heavy machinery operation in hazardous environments

812.rationale: Metal processing operators work in highly physical environments requiring manual dexterity, real-time monitoring of machinery, and hands-on adjustments to complex industrial equipment. While AI can optimize some parameters and provide predictive maintenance insights, the core tasks involve direct manipulation of physical materials, machinery operation requiring immediate tactile feedback, and quality inspection of physical products.
812.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, including predictive maintenance schedules or automated quality control systems that could impact job assignments.

813.rationale: Chemical plant operators work with complex industrial processes that generate substantial digital data streams from sensors, monitors, and control systems. AI can optimize process parameters, predict equipment failures, and automate routine monitoring tasks, though physical oversight and safety-critical interventions still require human presence.
813.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act high-risk classifications for industrial safety systems and GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making in hazardous environments create moderate barriers. Works councils will require consultation on AI systems affecting working conditions in chemical plants.

814.rationale: These machine operators work in manufacturing environments with standardized processes that AI can monitor and optimize, but require significant manual dexterity, real-time quality assessment, and hands-on machine adjustments. While AI can enhance process monitoring and quality control, the physical nature of handling materials and operating machinery creates natural barriers to full automation.
814.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, slowing implementation of automated monitoring systems. General employment protection laws create additional friction for workforce restructuring even where AI increases productivity.

815.rationale: These machine operators work with sophisticated industrial equipment that increasingly incorporates AI-driven automation for quality control, pattern optimization, and predictive maintenance. AI can enhance cutting precision, automate defect detection, optimize material usage, and predict machine failures, significantly transforming how these workers interact with their equipment.
815.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, and general employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring. However, this occupation group faces less regulatory friction than high-risk AI categories since manufacturing automation doesn't typically trigger EU AI Act Annex III classifications.
key_protected_tasks: adapting to custom orders and specifications

816.rationale: Food machine operators work in semi-structured physical environments with machinery that requires real-time monitoring, sensory evaluation (taste, smell, texture), and manual dexterity for handling ingredients and equipment. While AI can optimize some process parameters and quality control, the work involves significant physical manipulation and sensory assessment that current AI cannot fully replicate.
816.regulatory_friction: EU food safety regulations and GDPR create moderate friction for AI adoption in quality control and process optimization systems. Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG and ArbVG apply when AI systems change working conditions or productivity expectations.

817.rationale: Wood processing and papermaking plant operators primarily perform hands-on industrial tasks requiring real-time equipment monitoring, physical material handling, and safety-critical decision-making in manufacturing environments. While some process optimization and quality control aspects could benefit from AI assistance, the core work involves operating complex machinery with physical materials that require human oversight and manual intervention.
817.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, potentially slowing adoption of automated monitoring or optimization systems. General employment protection laws may also slow workforce restructuring even where AI enables productivity gains.
