818.rationale: These roles involve operating specialized industrial machinery in manufacturing environments, requiring physical presence, manual adjustments, and real-time monitoring of equipment. While some process optimization and predictive maintenance aspects could be enhanced by AI, the core work involves hands-on machine operation, quality control through physical inspection, and immediate responses to equipment conditions that require human presence and tactile feedback.
818.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, which could slow implementation of automated monitoring or performance evaluation systems. GDPR constraints on automated decision-making may limit AI-driven workforce management in manufacturing settings.
key_protected_tasks: real-time equipment monitoring and emergency response

821.rationale: Assembly work involves significant manual dexterity, physical manipulation of components, and real-time quality assessment in structured manufacturing environments. While some assembly processes can be automated with robotics and AI-guided systems, the work requires tactile feedback, spatial reasoning, and adaptation to physical variations that current AI struggles with.
821.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation before implementing AI systems that affect working conditions or performance monitoring. General employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring even where technical automation is possible.
key_protected_tasks: tactile assessment of part fit and alignment

831.rationale: While AI can automate some dispatching, scheduling, and monitoring tasks, locomotive operation requires real-time decision-making in complex physical environments, manual control of heavy machinery, and split-second safety responses. The physical nature of rail operations and safety-critical requirements create significant technical barriers to full automation.
831.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for safety-critical transport systems require extensive human oversight and approval processes. Works council co-determination rights in Germany/Austria will significantly slow any AI implementation affecting train operations and working conditions.

832.rationale: Professional driving involves complex real-time decision-making in dynamic physical environments, requiring spatial awareness, manual dexterity, and immediate responses to unpredictable situations. While autonomous vehicle technology is advancing, current AI still struggles with edge cases, bad weather, complex urban scenarios, and the varied customer service aspects of many driving roles.
832.regulatory_friction: EU transport regulations create significant barriers to autonomous vehicle deployment, requiring extensive safety testing and certification. Works councils must be consulted on any AI systems affecting driver working conditions, and employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring in traditional transport companies.

833.rationale: Heavy truck and bus drivers operate in complex, unstructured physical environments requiring real-time decision-making, manual dexterity, and human judgment for safety-critical situations. While autonomous vehicle technology exists, current AI capabilities cannot reliably handle the full spectrum of driving scenarios, vehicle maintenance checks, cargo handling, and passenger/animal welfare responsibilities these roles require.
833.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act classifies autonomous driving systems as high-risk applications requiring extensive safety validation. Transportation sector regulations, passenger safety requirements, and works council consultation rights for any AI affecting driving conditions create substantial deployment barriers.

834.rationale: Mobile plant operators work in highly physical, unstructured environments requiring real-time spatial awareness, manual dexterity, and safety judgment that current AI cannot replicate. While some telematics and automation exist, full autonomous operation of complex mobile machinery in dynamic construction/industrial sites remains technically challenging.
834.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act workplace monitoring provisions and works council co-determination rights (BetrVG §87) require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions. GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making also apply to any AI workforce management systems.

835.rationale: Ships' deck crews perform primarily physical, manual tasks in unstructured maritime environments requiring real-time human judgment for safety-critical operations. While some digital systems assist with navigation and monitoring, the core work involves hands-on deck operations, equipment maintenance, and emergency response that AI cannot directly perform.
835.regulatory_friction: Maritime work has minimal regulatory friction from EU AI Act since physical deck operations don't involve high-risk AI applications. Safety regulations focus on human competency requirements rather than restricting AI tools.
key_vulnerable_tasks: recording weather data and distance logs
key_protected_tasks: emergency damage control and safety operations

911.rationale: Cleaning work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, navigation of complex physical environments, and real-time adaptation to varied conditions. While some robotic cleaning systems exist (vacuums, floor cleaners), most cleaning tasks involve fine motor skills, problem-solving in unstructured spaces, and handling of varied surfaces and objects that current AI/robotics cannot reliably perform.
911.regulatory_friction: GDPR and works council consultation requirements may slow adoption of any AI-enabled cleaning management or scheduling systems. Employment protection laws provide additional friction against workforce restructuring, though the regulatory impact is limited since the technical barriers are already substantial.

912.rationale: These occupations require physical presence, manual dexterity, and work in unstructured environments with variable conditions. While some automation exists (car wash machines, pool cleaning robots), most tasks involve hands-on cleaning in spaces requiring human judgment for safety and quality.
912.regulatory_friction: Minimal regulatory friction as AI applications are limited and don't trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications or significant GDPR concerns.

921.rationale: Agricultural, forestry and fishery laborers work primarily in unstructured physical environments requiring manual dexterity, real-time adaptation to natural conditions, and hands-on care of living organisms. While some monitoring and data collection tasks could be AI-enhanced, the core work involves physical manipulation, animal handling, and navigation of unpredictable outdoor conditions that current AI cannot replicate.
921.regulatory_friction: EU employment protection laws and works council consultation requirements (BetrVG §87) slow adoption of AI systems that could affect working conditions. GDPR may limit automated monitoring of worker performance in outdoor settings.
key_protected_tasks: physical maintenance of equipment and facilities

931.rationale: Mining and construction labourers work in highly physical, unstructured outdoor environments requiring manual dexterity, heavy machinery operation, and real-time adaptation to site conditions. While some planning and monitoring tasks could benefit from AI assistance, the core physical construction, installation, and maintenance work requires human presence and cannot be directly automated by current AI systems.
931.regulatory_friction: Works council consultation requirements under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a apply to any AI systems affecting working conditions or safety monitoring. GDPR constraints limit automated workforce management and performance evaluation systems in construction contexts.

932.rationale: Manufacturing labourers work primarily in physical environments requiring manual dexterity, hand-eye coordination, and real-time adaptation to varied physical materials and conditions. While some tasks like inventory tracking or quality checking could benefit from AI assistance, the core work involves tactile skills and physical manipulation that current AI cannot replicate.
932.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 require consultation for any AI systems affecting working conditions, slowing adoption of AI-powered monitoring or task allocation systems in manufacturing environments.

933.rationale: Transport and storage laborers work primarily in physical environments requiring manual handling, spatial reasoning, and real-time problem-solving in unstructured settings. While some logistics coordination tasks could be AI-assisted, the core work involves physical manipulation of goods, human judgment for proper handling, and adaptation to varying physical conditions that current AI cannot directly perform.
933.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, particularly warehouse management systems. GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making may limit AI-driven task assignment and performance monitoring systems.
key_protected_tasks: manual handling and lifting of goods
real-time problem solving for damaged or irregular items

941.rationale: Food preparation assistants work primarily in physical environments requiring manual tasks like washing dishes, cleaning surfaces, basic food prep, and stock handling. While some ordering/inventory systems could be automated, the core work involves physical manipulation in unstructured kitchen environments with real-time coordination.
941.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated workforce scheduling and the EU AI Act's restrictions on AI systems for performance evaluation create modest friction. Works council rights in countries like Germany require consultation for any AI affecting working conditions.

951.rationale: Leaflet distribution requires physical presence in specific geographic locations, manual dexterity for handling materials, and real-time navigation of outdoor environments and human interactions. While route optimization could be AI-assisted, the core work is fundamentally physical and location-dependent.
951.regulatory_friction: Limited regulatory friction as this occupation rarely involves AI systems that would trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications or GDPR automated decision-making concerns. Basic employment protections may slow any workforce restructuring.

952.rationale: Street vendors work in unstructured physical environments requiring real-time human interaction, physical mobility, and manual handling of goods. While AI could assist with inventory management and payment processing, the core work of face-to-face selling, product demonstration, and navigating street locations requires human presence and adaptability.
952.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates minor friction around customer data collection and automated payment systems. Employment protection laws may slow adoption of AI-assisted vendor management systems in organized retail contexts.

961.rationale: Refuse work involves primarily physical labor in unstructured outdoor environments with manual handling of waste materials, limiting direct AI automation. Some sorting tasks could benefit from AI-powered optical sorting systems, but core collection and handling tasks require physical presence and dexterity.
961.regulatory_friction: GDPR may apply to any AI systems used for worker tracking or performance monitoring. General employment protections slow workforce restructuring, though this occupation group faces minimal high-risk AI Act classifications.

962.rationale: These elementary service occupations involve primarily physical tasks, manual labor, and real-time human interaction in unstructured environments. While some administrative aspects (like ticket checking or basic customer service) could be automated, the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and situational awareness that AI cannot easily replicate.
962.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 may limit automated decision-making for customer service interactions, and general employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring. However, since most tasks don't trigger AI Act high-risk classifications, regulatory friction is minimal.
key_protected_tasks: real-time security monitoring and emergency response
